schools.summary: Gill: this is the thing to be done to Aaron and his sons, to hallow them, to sanctify them, set them apart, and consecrate them to minister in the priest's office; the witnesses note God appointing the manner of hallowing Aaron and his sons to the priesthood, with a young bullock and two rams without blemish — the beginning of the great consecration, the sacrifices that must be offered (God ordaining the way the priests are set apart: 'this is the thing that thou shalt do unto them to hallow them... Take one young bullock, and two rams without blemish'; the end, 'to hallow them, to minister unto me in the priest's office,' that all this was done to sanctify them, set them apart, and consecrate them to serve God as priests; the victims, 'one young bullock, and two rams without blemish,' the bullock for a sin offering, one ram for a burnt offering, and the other the ram of consecration, all without blemish, perfect and unblemished; the type the commentators draw, that the consecration of Aaron and his sons shadows the consecrating of Christ, the great High Priest, and of believers, made priests unto God; that none could serve at the altar but such as were duly hallowed; that the unblemished victims point to Christ, the Lamb without blemish).

debates.question: What does the consecration of Aaron and his sons typify?

sides.position: The consecrating of Christ, the great High Priest, and of believers made priests unto God; the unblemished victims (bullock and two rams) pointing to Christ, the Lamb without blemish

themes: this is the thing that thou shalt do unto them to hallow them
Take one young bullock, and two rams without blemish
the opening of the rite to hallow Aaron and his sons to the priesthood
a type of the consecrating of Christ the High Priest and of believers, the unblemished victims pointing to Christ

schools.summary: Gill: unleavened bread, such as was eaten at the passover, distinguished from the cakes and wafers, all of wheaten flour; the witnesses note God appointing unleavened bread, cakes tempered with oil, and wafers anointed with oil, all of fine wheaten flour, for the rite — the meat-offering of the consecration, pure and mingled with oil (God adding the bread to the sacrifices: 'unleavened bread, and cakes unleavened tempered with oil, and wafers unleavened anointed with oil: of wheaten flour shalt thou make them'; the three sorts, unleavened bread, cakes tempered with oil, wafers anointed with oil, loaves and cakes and thin wafers, all of fine flour, the cakes mingled with oil and the wafers spread with it; that all was unleavened, free from leaven, the type of sin and corruption, that the offering might be pure; the oil, that the cakes and wafers were tempered and anointed with oil, the type of the Holy Spirit; the type the commentators draw, that the unleavened bread mingled with oil shadows Christ, the bread of life, pure and without the leaven of sin, full of the Spirit, and the saints, sincere and anointed; that the offering of God must be pure and Spirit-filled).

themes: unleavened bread, and cakes unleavened tempered with oil, and wafers unleavened anointed with oil
of wheaten flour shalt thou make them
the pure meat-offering of the consecration, unleavened and oiled
a type of Christ the bread of life, without the leaven of sin and full of the Spirit, and the anointed saints

schools.summary: Gill: the unleavened bread, cakes, and wafers were put into one basket, which may be an emblem of the Gospel and the ministration of it; the witnesses note God appointing the bread to be put into one basket and brought with the bullock and the two rams — the gathering of the offerings together for the rite (all brought ready for the consecration: 'thou shalt put them into one basket, and bring them in the basket, with the bullock and the two rams'; the basket, 'put them into one basket,' that the three sorts of bread were gathered into one basket and so brought; the bringing, 'bring them in the basket, with the bullock and the two rams,' that the bread in the basket and the three victims were all brought together to the place of consecration, ready for the rite; the emblem some commentators draw, that the one basket holding the bread may be an emblem of the Gospel and the ministration of it, in which Christ, the bread of life, is held forth and offered; the order, that all the materials were prepared and brought beforehand, nothing wanting; that God would have all things ready for his service).

themes: thou shalt put them into one basket, and bring them in the basket
with the bullock and the two rams
the bread gathered into one basket and brought with the victims, all made ready
some seeing the basket an emblem of the Gospel, in which Christ the bread of life is held forth

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was to order and direct them to come to the door of the tabernacle, and there to wash them with water; the witnesses note God appointing Aaron and his sons to be brought to the door of the tabernacle and washed with water — the first act of consecration, the washing, the cleansing before service (the priests must first be washed: 'Aaron and his sons thou shalt bring unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and shalt wash them with water'; the place, 'the door of the tabernacle of the congregation,' that they were brought publicly to the entrance, before the people, where the rite was done; the first act, 'shalt wash them with water,' that, before they were clothed or anointed, they were washed all over with water, signifying cleansing; the great type the commentators draw, that this washing shadows the cleansing needful before any may serve God, the washing of regeneration and of the word, and that Christ, our High Priest, was holy and needed no cleansing, but his people must be washed; that none may approach God's service unwashed and unclean; that the washing went before the robing and anointing, cleansing first).

themes: Aaron and his sons thou shalt bring unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation
and shalt wash them with water
the first act of consecration, the priests washed with water before being clothed or anointed
a type of the washing of regeneration and of the word, none may approach God's service unwashed

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: as most officers had a peculiar dress, so the priests were clothed with their official garments at their consecration; the witnesses note God appointing Aaron to be clothed with the coat, the robe of the ephod, the ephod, and the breastplate, and girded with the curious girdle — the robing of the high priest in his holy garments after the washing (being washed, he is clothed for his office: 'thou shalt take the garments, and put upon Aaron the coat, and the robe of the ephod, and the ephod, and the breastplate, and gird him with the curious girdle of the ephod'; the order, that the washing went before, and now the robing, the clean priest clothed; the garments named, the coat, the robe of the ephod, the ephod, the breastplate, and the curious girdle, the holy and beautiful vestments before appointed, each with its meaning; that, as most officers had a peculiar dress, so the priest was invested with the garments of his office, marking him set apart; the type the commentators draw, that the holy garments shadow the righteousness and graces with which Christ, our High Priest, and his people are clothed for God's service; that, washed and then robed, the priest was fitted to draw near; that we must be clothed with righteousness to serve God).

themes: thou shalt take the garments, and put upon Aaron the coat, and the robe of the ephod, and the ephod, and the breastplate
and gird him with the curious girdle of the ephod
Aaron, being washed, then clothed with the holy garments of his office
a type of the righteousness and graces with which Christ our High Priest and his people are clothed

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the head-dress of the high priest was the mitre, upon which was set the holy crown, the golden plate engraved Holiness to the LORD; the witnesses note God appointing the mitre to be put on Aaron's head and the holy crown upon the mitre — the crowning of the high priest with the mitre and the golden plate of holiness (the priest crowned with holiness: 'thou shalt put the mitre upon his head, and put the holy crown upon the mitre'; the mitre, 'the mitre upon his head,' the linen turban, the high priest's head-dress, a wrap of linen about his head; the holy crown, 'the holy crown upon the mitre,' the golden plate fastened on the front of the mitre, engraved Holiness to the LORD, called the holy crown; that this crowned the high priest, marking him as wholly consecrated, holiness to the LORD set upon his very forehead; the type the commentators draw, that the golden crown of holiness shadows Christ, our High Priest, who is holiness itself, crowned with glory and holiness, and that his people too are to be holy; that holiness becomes God's house and his priests; that the high priest bore holiness as his crown).

themes: thou shalt put the mitre upon his head
and put the holy crown upon the mitre
the mitre and the golden crown of holiness ('Holiness to the LORD') set on Aaron's head
a type of Christ our High Priest, who is holiness itself, crowned with glory and holiness

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the anointing oil, made of principal spices, myrrh, cinnamon, calamus, cassia, and oil olive, was poured upon his head; the witnesses note God appointing the anointing oil to be poured upon Aaron's head, to anoint him — the anointing of the high priest, the pouring of the holy oil, the type of the Spirit (washed and robed and crowned, he is now anointed: 'shalt thou take the anointing oil, and pour it upon his head, and anoint him'; the oil, 'the anointing oil,' the holy oil made of principal spices, myrrh, cinnamon, calamus, cassia, and oil olive, set apart for this use; the act, 'pour it upon his head, and anoint him,' that the oil was poured plentifully on Aaron's head, running down, as in the psalm, anointing him to his office; the great type the commentators draw, that the anointing oil shadows the Holy Spirit, with which Christ, the Anointed One, Messiah, Christ, was anointed without measure, and his people anointed in him; that the high priest was set apart to his office by the anointing, as Christ to his by the Spirit; that none serves God aright but by the anointing of the Spirit).

debates.question: What does the anointing oil poured on Aaron's head typify?

sides.position: The Holy Spirit — with which Christ, the Anointed One (Messiah, Christ), was anointed without measure, and his people anointed in him; the high priest set apart to his office by the anointing, as Christ to his by the Spirit

themes: Then shalt thou take the anointing oil, and pour it upon his head
and anoint him
the holy anointing oil poured plentifully on Aaron's head, anointing him to his office
the great type of the Holy Spirit, with which Christ the Anointed One was anointed without measure

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was to order the sons of Aaron to come to the same place where he was, and to put coats upon them; the witnesses note God appointing Aaron's sons to be brought and clothed with coats — the robing of the common priests, the sons clothed after the high priest (the sons too clothed for their office: 'thou shalt bring his sons, and put coats upon them'; the bringing, 'bring his sons,' that the sons of Aaron were brought to the same place, to be consecrated with their father; the clothing, 'put coats upon them,' that they too were clothed, though with the plainer garments of the common priests, the coats, not the high priest's full vestments; the distinction, that Aaron, the high priest, had the full and glorious garments, the mitre, crown, and anointing, while his sons had the coats, each according to his office; the type the commentators draw, that as the sons were clothed and consecrated with the father, so all believers, the spiritual priesthood, are clothed and consecrated in Christ, their great High Priest; that all God's priests, high and common, are clothed for his service).

themes: thou shalt bring his sons
and put coats upon them
Aaron's sons brought and clothed with coats, the common priests robed after the high priest
a type of all believers, the spiritual priesthood, clothed and consecrated in Christ their great High Priest

schools.summary: Gill: Aaron was girded with the girdle of the ephod and of needlework, and his sons with common girdles, all showing what strength, diligence, and expedition were necessary for their office; the witnesses note God appointing Aaron and his sons to be girded and have bonnets put on, the priesthood made theirs for a perpetual statute, and so consecrated — the completing of the robing and the granting of the office (the priesthood settled on Aaron's house: 'thou shalt gird them with girdles... and put the bonnets on them: and the priest's office shall be theirs for a perpetual statute: and thou shalt consecrate Aaron and his sons'; the girding, 'gird them with girdles,' Aaron with his own and his sons with common girdles, the girdle showing what strength, diligence, and expedition were necessary for their service; the bonnets, 'put the bonnets on them,' the head-dress of the priests; the grant, 'the priest's office shall be theirs for a perpetual statute,' that the priesthood was settled on Aaron and his line for ever, by a lasting ordinance; the word, 'consecrate Aaron and his sons,' in the Hebrew fill their hands, to consecrate being to fill the hand, with the offerings, the mark of investiture; the type, that the everlasting priesthood points to Christ's, a priesthood unchangeable).

themes: thou shalt gird them with girdles, Aaron and his sons, and put the bonnets on them
and the priest's office shall be theirs for a perpetual statute
the priests girded and the office settled on Aaron's house for ever
to 'consecrate' being in the Hebrew to 'fill the hand,' the everlasting priesthood pointing to Christ's unchangeable priesthood

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: their putting their hands upon the head of the bullock was their identifying themselves with the animal, transferring as it were their guilt to it; the witnesses note God appointing the bullock to be brought and Aaron and his sons to lay their hands on its head — the presenting of the sin offering, the priests identifying themselves with the victim (laying their hands on the bullock that was to die for them: 'thou shalt cause a bullock to be brought before the tabernacle of the congregation: and Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head of the bullock'; the bringing, 'a bullock to be brought before the tabernacle,' the bullock for the sin offering, brought to the door; the laying on of hands, 'Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head of the bullock,' that, by this act, they were identifying themselves with the animal, transferring as it were their guilt to it, the victim standing in their stead; the great type the commentators draw, that the laying on of hands shadows the transfer of guilt to a substitute, fulfilled in Christ, on whom the LORD laid the iniquity of us all, the sins of his people imputed to him; that even the priests, before they could serve, must have their own sin atoned by a substitute; that none comes to God but by a sin-bearing sacrifice).

debates.question: What does the laying on of hands upon the bullock's head signify?

sides.position: The identifying of the offerer with the victim and the transfer of guilt to a substitute — fulfilled in Christ, on whom the LORD laid the iniquity of us all (Isaiah 53:6), the sins of his people imputed to him; even the priests must have their own sin atoned by a substitute

themes: thou shalt cause a bullock to be brought before the tabernacle of the congregation
and Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head of the bullock
the priests laying their hands on the bullock, identifying with the victim and transferring their guilt
a type of the transfer of guilt to a substitute, fulfilled in Christ, on whom the LORD laid the iniquity of us all

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was ordered to do it, who now officiated as a priest pro tempore, Aaron and his sons not yet completely invested, this sacrifice being part of their consecration; the witnesses note God appointing the bullock to be killed before the LORD by the door of the tabernacle, Moses doing it — the slaying of the sin offering, the death of the victim before the LORD (the substitute put to death: 'thou shalt kill the bullock before the LORD, by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation'; the act, 'thou shalt kill the bullock,' that Moses, who now officiated as a priest pro tempore, the priests not yet fully consecrated, slew the victim; the place, 'before the LORD, by the door of the tabernacle,' at the entrance, in God's presence, where the offerings were made; that the bullock, on which they had laid their hands, was now put to death in their stead, the wages of sin being death; the type the commentators draw, that the death of the sin offering shadows the death of Christ, the substitute, who died for the sins of his people, the just for the unjust; that without the shedding of blood there is no remission; that the priests' own sin must be expiated by the death of a victim).

themes: thou shalt kill the bullock before the LORD
by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation
the bullock killed before the LORD in their stead, Moses officiating for the time
a type of the death of Christ the substitute, who died for the sins of his people, without shedding of blood no remission

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the virtue of the altar was considered to reside especially in the horns, and so the blood was put upon them; the witnesses note God appointing the blood of the bullock to be put on the horns of the altar with the finger and the rest poured at the bottom — the applying of the atoning blood to the altar, the cleansing of the place of sacrifice (the blood set on the horns and poured at the base: 'thou shalt take of the blood of the bullock, and put it upon the horns of the altar with thy finger, and pour all the blood beside the bottom of the altar'; the horns, 'put it upon the horns of the altar with thy finger,' that some of the blood was put on the four horns of the altar, where the virtue of the altar was considered to reside especially, the horns being the chief and highest points; the rest, 'pour all the blood beside the bottom of the altar,' that the remainder was poured out at the base; that the blood thus consecrated and cleansed the altar, making atonement for it, that it might be fit for the offerings; the type the commentators draw, that the blood applied shadows the blood of Christ, by which the conscience and the worship are cleansed and atonement made; that all is sanctified by blood).

themes: thou shalt take of the blood of the bullock, and put it upon the horns of the altar with thy finger
and pour all the blood beside the bottom of the altar
the atoning blood put on the horns of the altar and poured at the base, cleansing and consecrating it
a type of the blood of Christ, by which the conscience and worship are cleansed and atonement made

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: unlike a whole burnt offering, in the sin offering only the fat and certain inward parts were burnt upon the altar; the witnesses note God appointing the fat, the caul above the liver, the two kidneys and their fat, to be burnt upon the altar — the burning of the choicest inward parts to God, the fat as his portion (the best of the inwards given to God: 'thou shalt take all the fat that covereth the inwards, and the caul that is above the liver, and the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them, and burn them upon the altar'; the parts, 'all the fat... the caul... the two kidneys, and the fat,' the fat covering the inwards and the choice inner parts, accounted the richest and best; the burning, 'burn them upon the altar,' that these were burnt to God on the altar, the fat being his special portion, the fat is the LORD's; that in the sin offering only the fat and inwards were burnt on the altar, the rest burnt without the camp; the meaning the commentators draw, that the burning of the fat, the best, shadows the giving of the choicest to God, the inward and hidden parts, the heart and affections, yielded up to him; that God claims the best and the inmost).

themes: thou shalt take all the fat that covereth the inwards, and the caul that is above the liver, and the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them
and burn them upon the altar
the fat and choice inward parts burnt to God on the altar, the fat his special portion
some seeing the inward parts shadow the heart and affections yielded to God, who claims the best

schools.summary: Gill: the flesh, with the head, legs, and feet, the skin, and the dung, were burnt with fire without the camp, the whole being a sin offering; the witnesses note God appointing the flesh, skin, and dung of the bullock to be burnt with fire without the camp, because it was a sin offering — the burning of the body of the sin offering outside the camp, the sin borne away (the great type of Christ suffering without the gate: 'the flesh of the bullock, and his skin, and his dung, shalt thou burn with fire without the camp: it is a sin offering'; the parts, 'the flesh... his skin, and his dung,' the whole body of the bullock, all but the fat and inwards burnt on the altar; the place, 'burn... without the camp,' not on the altar, but carried outside the camp and there consumed with fire; the reason, 'it is a sin offering,' that, as bearing sin, the body was burnt away without, the sin removed from the holy place; the great type the commentators draw, that as the bodies of the sin offerings were burnt without the camp, so Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate, bearing our sin and reproach; that the sin-bearer is cast out, that the people may be cleansed).

debates.question: What does burning the sin offering's body 'without the camp' typify?

sides.position: Christ suffering without the gate (Hebrews 13:11-12) — as the bodies of the sin offerings were burnt without the camp, so Jesus, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate, bearing our sin and reproach, the sin-bearer cast out that the people may be cleansed

themes: the flesh of the bullock, and his skin, and his dung, shalt thou burn with fire without the camp
it is a sin offering
the body of the sin offering burnt without the camp, the sin borne away
the great type of Christ suffering without the gate, bearing our sin and reproach to sanctify the people

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the one ram, one of the two already mentioned, on whose head they again put their hands, identifying themselves with the animal as before; the witnesses note God appointing one ram to be taken and Aaron and his sons to lay their hands on its head — the presenting of the burnt offering, the priests again identifying with the victim (laying their hands on the ram of the burnt offering: 'Thou shalt also take one ram; and Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head of the ram'; the victim, 'one ram,' the first of the two rams, now for a burnt offering; the laying on of hands, 'Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head of the ram,' again identifying themselves with the animal, as with the bullock, the victim standing for them; the difference of this offering, that the sin offering atoned for guilt, but the burnt offering, wholly consumed, signified entire devotion and consecration to God; the type the commentators draw, that the burnt offering shadows Christ's entire offering of himself to God, a sweet savour, and the believer's whole devotion to God; that, sin first atoned, the priest is wholly given up to God; that we must yield ourselves wholly to him).

themes: Thou shalt also take one ram
and Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head of the ram
the first ram for a burnt offering, the priests identifying with the victim
the burnt offering signifying entire devotion to God, a type of Christ's whole offering and the believer's

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the act intended is the throwing of the blood from a basin against the lower part of the altar, round about; the witnesses note God appointing the ram to be slain and its blood sprinkled round about upon the altar — the slaying of the burnt offering and the casting of its blood about the altar (the blood dashed round the altar: 'thou shalt slay the ram, and thou shalt take his blood, and sprinkle it round about upon the altar'; the slaying, 'thou shalt slay the ram,' that Moses, as before, killed the victim of the burnt offering; the blood, 'sprinkle it round about upon the altar,' better, that the blood was scattered or thrown from a basin against the lower part of the altar, round about, cast all about it; the difference from the sin offering's blood, put on the horns, that here the blood of the burnt offering was simply dashed round the altar; that the blood, the life, was thus offered to God all about his altar, the whole devoted; the type, that the blood of the burnt offering shadows the blood of Christ, who offered himself wholly to God, his blood the ground of acceptance; that the offering is presented to God in its blood).

themes: thou shalt slay the ram, and thou shalt take his blood
and sprinkle it round about upon the altar
the ram of the burnt offering slain, its blood dashed round about the altar
a type of the blood of Christ, who offered himself wholly to God, his blood the ground of acceptance

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the cutting of the ram in pieces was the ordinary practice, not only among the Hebrews but among other nations, for the burnt offering; the witnesses note God appointing the ram to be cut in pieces, the inwards and legs washed and laid with the pieces and head — the preparing of the burnt offering for the altar, all made clean and ready (the whole victim washed and set in order: 'thou shalt cut the ram in pieces, and wash the inwards of him, and his legs, and put them unto his pieces, and unto his head'; the cutting, 'cut the ram in pieces,' that the ram was divided for the better convenience of laying it upon the wood on the altar, that it might be wholly burnt; the washing, 'wash the inwards of him, and his legs,' that the inner parts and legs were washed clean, that the whole offering might be pure; the ordering, 'put them unto his pieces, and unto his head,' that all, washed and cut, was laid together in order on the altar; the meaning the commentators draw, that the washing of the inwards shadows the purity of the inner man, the heart and walk, the legs, cleansed, that the whole offering be holy; that what is offered to God must be clean within and without).

themes: thou shalt cut the ram in pieces, and wash the inwards of him, and his legs
and put them unto his pieces, and unto his head
the ram cut, its inwards and legs washed, and all laid in order for the altar
some seeing the washing shadow the purity of the inner man, clean within and without

schools.summary: Gill: the whole ram was burnt upon the altar, his head, pieces, inwards, and legs laid in order, for it was a whole burnt offering; the witnesses note God appointing the whole ram to be burnt upon the altar, a burnt offering of a sweet savour to the LORD — the entire consuming of the burnt offering, wholly given to God and well-pleasing (the whole burnt to God: 'thou shalt burn the whole ram upon the altar: it is a burnt offering unto the LORD: it is a sweet savour, an offering made by fire unto the LORD'; the whole, 'burn the whole ram upon the altar,' that, unlike the sin offering, the burnt offering was wholly consumed on the altar, head, pieces, inwards, and legs together; the kind, 'it is a burnt offering unto the LORD,' wholly given up to God, nothing reserved; the acceptance, 'a sweet savour, an offering made by fire unto the LORD,' that the burning was a sweet savour, well-pleasing and accepted of God; the great type the commentators draw, that the whole burnt offering shadows Christ's entire offering of himself to God, a sweetsmelling savour, wholly devoted, wholly accepted, and the believer's whole consecration; that what is wholly given to God is a sweet savour to him).

debates.question: What does the whole burnt offering, a sweet savour, typify?

sides.position: Christ's entire offering of himself to God — 'a sweetsmelling savour' (Ephesians 5:2), wholly devoted and wholly accepted — and the believer's whole consecration; what is wholly given to God is a sweet savour to him

themes: thou shalt burn the whole ram upon the altar: it is a burnt offering unto the LORD
it is a sweet savour, an offering made by fire unto the LORD
the whole ram burnt on the altar, wholly given to God, a sweet savour and well-pleasing
the great type of Christ's entire offering, 'a sweetsmelling savour,' and the believer's whole consecration

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the other ram was the ram of consecration, eil milluim, the ram of filling up, because when a person was consecrated his hands were filled; the witnesses note God appointing the other ram to be taken and Aaron and his sons to lay their hands on its head — the presenting of the ram of consecration, the chief offering of the rite (the ram of filling brought: 'thou shalt take the other ram; and Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head of the ram'; the victim, 'the other ram,' the second of the two, the ram of consecration, in the Hebrew the ram of filling up, so called because when a person was consecrated his hands were filled with the offerings; the laying on of hands, 'Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head,' again identifying themselves with the victim, as with the bullock and the first ram; the special place of this offering, that it was the proper consecration sacrifice, by which the priests were installed and their hands filled; the type the commentators draw, that the ram of consecration, the ram of filling, shadows Christ, who fills his people's hands with himself and his gifts, by whom they are consecrated and installed in their priesthood; that none is consecrated to God but through the appointed sacrifice).

themes: thou shalt take the other ram
and Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head of the ram
the ram of consecration ('the ram of filling up'), the proper consecration sacrifice
a type of Christ, who fills his people's hands with himself and his gifts, by whom they are consecrated

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the putting the blood on the tip of the right ear, the thumb of the right hand, and the great toe of the right foot was intended to signify that they should dedicate all their faculties and powers to the service of God; the witnesses note God appointing the blood of the ram to be put on the right ear, right thumb, and right great toe of the priests, and sprinkled round the altar — the touching of the priests with blood at ear, hand, and foot, the whole man consecrated (the blood claiming all their powers for God: 'put it upon the tip of the right ear of Aaron... and upon the thumb of their right hand, and upon the great toe of their right foot'; the three places, the tip of the right ear, the thumb of the right hand, the great toe of the right foot, the chief parts of hearing, doing, and walking; the great meaning the commentators draw, that this was to signify that they should dedicate all their faculties and powers to the service of God, the ear that they might hear his word, the hand that they might do his work, the foot that they might walk in his ways; that the whole man, sanctified by blood, was claimed for God, what he hears, does, and walks; the type, that the blood of Christ sanctifies the whole man, ear, hand, and foot, the believer wholly given up to God; that all our powers are to be devoted to him).

debates.question: What does the blood on the right ear, thumb, and great toe signify?

sides.position: That the priests should dedicate all their faculties and powers to God's service — the ear to hear his word, the hand to do his work, the foot to walk in his ways; the whole man, sanctified by blood, claimed for God, a type of the blood of Christ sanctifying the whole man

themes: put it upon the tip of the right ear of Aaron, and upon the thumb of their right hand, and upon the great toe of their right foot
and sprinkle the blood upon the altar round about
the blood put on ear, hand, and foot, dedicating all the priests' faculties to God
the ear to hear, the hand to do, the foot to walk; the whole man sanctified by blood, a type of the blood of Christ

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the blood and the anointing oil were taken together and sprinkled upon Aaron and his sons and their garments, to hallow both the persons and the vestments; the witnesses note God appointing the blood from the altar and the anointing oil to be sprinkled on Aaron and his sons and their garments, that they might be hallowed — the joining of blood and oil, the hallowing of the priests by atonement and the Spirit (blood and oil together sanctify them: 'take of the blood that is upon the altar, and of the anointing oil, and sprinkle it upon Aaron, and upon his garments, and upon his sons... and he shall be hallowed'; the two together, 'of the blood... and of the anointing oil,' that the atoning blood and the anointing oil were mingled and sprinkled together; the sprinkling, 'upon Aaron, and upon his garments, and upon his sons,' that both the persons and the garments were sprinkled; the result, 'he shall be hallowed, and his garments, and his sons,' that all were thereby consecrated, made holy; the great meaning the commentators draw, that the blood and the oil together shadow the two great means of consecration, the blood of Christ, atonement, and the Spirit, anointing, by which alone God's people and their service are hallowed; that we are sanctified by blood and Spirit).

debates.question: What do the blood and the anointing oil sprinkled together signify?

sides.position: The two great means of consecration — the blood of Christ (atonement) and the Spirit (anointing) — by which alone God's people and their service are hallowed; we are sanctified by blood and Spirit

themes: take of the blood that is upon the altar, and of the anointing oil, and sprinkle it upon Aaron, and upon his garments
and he shall be hallowed, and his garments, and his sons, and his sons' garments with him
the atoning blood and the anointing oil sprinkled together on the priests and garments, to hallow them
the two great means of consecration, the blood of Christ and the Spirit, by which God's people are sanctified

schools.summary: Gill: the fat and the rump, the large fat tail of the sheep in those parts, and the inward fat, and the right shoulder, were taken, for it was a ram of consecration; the witnesses note God appointing the fat, the rump, the inward parts, and the right shoulder of the consecration ram to be taken — the choice parts of the ram of consecration set apart for the rite (the best taken for the filling of the hands: 'take of the ram the fat and the rump... and the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them, and the right shoulder; for it is a ram of consecration'; the parts, 'the fat and the rump... the caul above the liver... the two kidneys... and the right shoulder,' the rich fat, the fat tail, the sheep in Arabia and about Judea being remarkable for their large tails, the inward fat, and the right shoulder, all the choicest portions; the difference, that here, unlike the other offerings, the right shoulder too was taken, the priest's portion; the reason, 'for it is a ram of consecration,' that, being the consecration sacrifice, its choice parts were used to fill the priests' hands; that the best and choicest of the offering was given to this special rite; that God claims the best in the consecration of his servants).

themes: Also thou shalt take of the ram the fat and the rump, and the fat that covereth the inwards, and the caul above the liver, and the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them, and the right shoulder
for it is a ram of consecration
the choicest parts of the consecration ram taken for the rite, to fill the priests' hands
God claiming the best in the consecration of his servants

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the bread of different kinds, in this offering, seems to have been intended as a minchah, or offering of grateful acknowledgment to God; the witnesses note God appointing one loaf, one cake of oiled bread, and one wafer to be taken from the basket of unleavened bread before the LORD — the joining of the bread to the offering, the meat-offering with the flesh (the bread added to the ram's portions: 'one loaf of bread, and one cake of oiled bread, and one wafer out of the basket of the unleavened bread that is before the LORD'; the three sorts, 'one loaf... one cake of oiled bread... one wafer,' one of each kind from the basket, taken with the choice parts of the ram; the meaning the commentators draw, that the bread, intended as a minchah, or offering of grateful acknowledgment to God, was joined with the flesh, the meat-offering with the sacrifice; that the bread and the flesh together filled the priests' hands; the type, that the bread, the meat-offering of thanksgiving, with the ram, shadows Christ, the bread of life and the sacrifice, and the thankful devotion of his people; that thanksgiving accompanies the offering).

themes: one loaf of bread, and one cake of oiled bread, and one wafer
out of the basket of the unleavened bread that is before the LORD
one of each bread joined to the ram's choice parts, a meat-offering of grateful acknowledgment to God
a type of Christ the bread of life and the sacrifice, and the thankful devotion of his people

schools.summary: Gill: the things of the ram, bread, cakes, and wafers were put into their hands when consecrated, which accounts for the phrase filling the hand for consecration, denoting their investiture with their office; the witnesses note God appointing all the portions to be put in the hands of Aaron and his sons and waved before the LORD — the filling of the priests' hands, the wave offering, the very act of consecration (their hands filled and the offering waved: 'put all in the hands of Aaron, and in the hands of his sons; and shalt wave them for a wave offering before the LORD'; the filling, 'put all in the hands of Aaron, and... his sons,' that the choice parts of the ram and the bread were put into their hands, which accounts for the phrase filling the hand for consecration, the very mark of their investiture with their office; the waving, 'wave them for a wave offering before the LORD,' that they, with the offerings in their hands, waved them to and fro before the LORD, presenting them to God; the meaning the commentators draw, that the filling of the hands signified the giving to them of their priestly office and its service, their hands now full of the things of God; the type, that Christ fills his people's hands with himself and his gifts, consecrating them to their priesthood; that the consecrated hand is a hand filled by God).

debates.question: What does 'filling the hand' (the wave offering put in the priests' hands) signify?

sides.position: The very act of consecration — whence in the Hebrew 'to consecrate' is 'to fill the hand'; the offerings put into their hands and waved before the LORD denote their investiture with their office, a type of Christ filling his people's hands with himself and his gifts

themes: thou shalt put all in the hands of Aaron, and in the hands of his sons
and shalt wave them for a wave offering before the LORD
the offerings put in the priests' hands and waved, the very act of consecration ('filling the hand')
a type of Christ filling his people's hands with himself and his gifts, consecrating them to their priesthood

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: Aaron and his sons are here considered merely as common persons bringing an offering to God, not yet having authority to present it themselves, so Moses received it of their hands; the witnesses note God appointing the offerings to be received from the priests' hands and burnt on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD — the burning of the wave offering, the consecration completed in fire (what was waved now burnt to God: 'thou shalt receive them of their hands, and burn them upon the altar for a burnt offering, for a sweet savour before the LORD'; the receiving, 'receive them of their hands,' that Moses, the priests not yet having authority to present it themselves, took the offerings from their filled hands; the burning, 'burn them upon the altar for a burnt offering,' that the waved portions were then burnt on the altar, wholly given to God; the acceptance, 'a sweet savour before the LORD,' well-pleasing and accepted, as the burnt offering before; that the consecration offering, having filled their hands and been waved, was at last consumed in fire to God; the type, that all our consecration ends in being wholly given up to God, a sweet savour through Christ; that what is consecrated is offered up entire to God).

themes: thou shalt receive them of their hands, and burn them upon the altar for a burnt offering, for a sweet savour before the LORD
it is an offering made by fire unto the LORD
the offerings received from the priests' hands and burnt on the altar, the consecration completed in fire
all our consecration ending in being wholly given up to God, a sweet savour through Christ

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: it was the general law that in wave offerings the breast should be the officiating priest's part; the witnesses note God appointing the breast of the consecration ram to be waved before the LORD and given to Moses as his part — the breast set apart as the portion of the officiating priest, here Moses (the wave breast Moses's portion: 'thou shalt take the breast of the ram of Aaron's consecration, and wave it for a wave offering before the LORD: and it shall be thy part'; the breast, 'the breast of the ram of Aaron's consecration,' that this part of the ram was particularly disposed of; the waving, 'wave it for a wave offering before the LORD,' moved to and fro before God, presenting it to him and receiving it back; the portion, 'it shall be thy part,' that, the breast being the officiating priest's part, it fell to Moses, who served as priest in this rite; the order, that God provides a portion for him that serves at the altar; the type, that they who serve God are fed from his altar and partake of the offering; that God gives his ministers their portion).

themes: thou shalt take the breast of the ram of Aaron's consecration, and wave it for a wave offering before the LORD
and it shall be thy part
the wave breast given to Moses, the officiating priest, as his part
God providing a portion for him that serves at the altar, they who serve God fed from his altar

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: as the wave offering was agitated to and fro, and the heave offering up and down, the breast and shoulder were thereby set apart as the priest's portion; the witnesses note God appointing the breast of the wave offering and the shoulder of the heave offering to be sanctified, the portion of Aaron and his sons — the setting apart of the breast and shoulder as the priests' perpetual portion (breast and shoulder hallowed for the priests: 'thou shalt sanctify the breast of the wave offering, and the shoulder of the heave offering, which is waved, and which is heaved up... for Aaron, and... his sons'; the two parts, 'the breast of the wave offering, and the shoulder of the heave offering,' the breast, waved to and fro, and the shoulder, heaved up and down, the two motions distinguishing them; the sanctifying, 'thou shalt sanctify,' that these were set apart for the use of the priest in all succeeding ages; that the breast, waved, and the shoulder, heaved, were henceforth the priests' due portion of the peace offerings; the meaning some draw, that the breast, the seat of love and affection, and the shoulder, the seat of strength, were the priest's, that he might have the love and the strength of the offering; that God provides for his priests of the offerings).

themes: thou shalt sanctify the breast of the wave offering, and the shoulder of the heave offering, which is waved, and which is heaved up
even of that which is for Aaron, and of that which is for his sons
the breast (waved) and shoulder (heaved) sanctified as the priests' perpetual portion
some seeing the breast (love) and shoulder (strength) given to the priest, God providing of the offerings

schools.summary: Gill: the shoulder, particularly meant, was Aaron's and his sons' by an everlasting statute, an ordinance in Israel, the heave offering from the peace offerings; the witnesses note God making the breast and shoulder the perpetual portion of the priests, a heave offering from the people's peace offerings to the LORD — the lasting provision for the priesthood out of the people's offerings (the priests' portion settled for ever: 'it shall be Aaron's and his sons' by a statute for ever... an heave offering from the children of Israel of the sacrifice of their peace offerings'; the grant, 'Aaron's and his sons' by a statute for ever,' that this portion was theirs by an everlasting statute and ordinance in Israel, through all generations; the source, 'an heave offering from the children of Israel of... their peace offerings,' that, whenever the people offered peace offerings, the breast and shoulder were heaved up to the LORD and given to the priests; the meaning, that God thus provided a settled maintenance for his priests out of the offerings of the people; the type, that they who minister at the altar live of the altar, God ordaining that his servants be supported; that God cares for the upkeep of his ministers).

themes: it shall be Aaron's and his sons' by a statute for ever from the children of Israel: for it is an heave offering
even their heave offering unto the LORD
the breast and shoulder made the priests' portion for ever from the people's peace offerings
God providing a settled maintenance for his priests, they who minister at the altar live of the altar

schools.summary: Gill: the holy garments of Aaron were to be his son's after him, that son that succeeded him in the priesthood, for the priesthood continued in Aaron's family by succession; the witnesses note God appointing the holy garments to pass to Aaron's successor, to be anointed and consecrated in them — the succession of the high priesthood, the garments handed down (the holy garments descending to the successor: 'the holy garments of Aaron shall be his sons' after him, to be anointed therein, and to be consecrated in them'; the succession, 'his sons' after him,' that the garments passed to that son that succeeded him in the priesthood, the priesthood continuing in Aaron's family by succession, the eldest son being high priest; the use, 'to be anointed therein, and to be consecrated in them,' that the successor was anointed and consecrated wearing these same holy garments, as Aaron had been; the meaning, that the office and its robes passed on, the high priesthood continuing through the line; the type the commentators draw, that the changing succession of mortal high priests, who died and passed on their garments, points by contrast to Christ, who, because he continueth ever, hath an unchangeable priesthood, needing no successor; that the Aaronic line was passing, but Christ's priesthood abides).

debates.question: What does the succession of the high priest's garments point to?

sides.position: By contrast, Christ's unchangeable priesthood — the mortal high priests died and passed on their garments to a successor, but Christ, because he continueth ever, hath an unchangeable priesthood (Hebrews 7:23-24), needing no successor

themes: the holy garments of Aaron shall be his sons' after him
to be anointed therein, and to be consecrated in them
the holy garments passing to Aaron's successor, the high priesthood continuing through the line
the changing succession of mortal high priests pointing by contrast to Christ's unchangeable priesthood

schools.summary: Gill: the high priest was to be of the family of Aaron, a descendant of his; it was not enough that he was of the tribe of Levi, but he must descend from Aaron; the witnesses note God appointing the successor to put on the holy garments seven days when he came to minister in the holy place — the seven days' wearing of the garments by the new high priest, the full consecration (the successor consecrated through seven days: 'that son that is priest in his stead shall put them on seven days, when he cometh into the tabernacle... to minister in the holy place'; the successor, 'that son that is priest in his stead,' the one of Aaron's line who succeeded to the high priesthood, who must descend from Aaron, not merely be of Levi; the seven days, 'shall put them on seven days,' that the new high priest wore the holy garments seven days together at his entering on his office, the full time of consecration; the purpose, 'when he cometh into the tabernacle... to minister in the holy place,' that, so consecrated, he came to do the priestly service; the meaning, that the seven days, the perfect number, marked a complete and solemn consecration before he ministered; that God would have his servants fully consecrated before they serve).

themes: that son that is priest in his stead shall put them on seven days
when he cometh into the tabernacle of the congregation to minister in the holy place
the successor wearing the holy garments seven days as he came to minister
the seven days marking a complete consecration, God having his servants fully consecrated before they serve

schools.summary: John Wesley: the other part of the flesh of the ram, and of the bread, Aaron and his sons were to eat at the door of the tabernacle, to signify that God not only called them but fed them at his table; the witnesses note God appointing the ram of consecration to be taken and its flesh boiled in the holy place — the preparing of the consecration flesh for the priests to eat (the flesh made ready for the holy meal: 'thou shalt take the ram of the consecration, and seethe his flesh in the holy place'; that this was the part of the ram not burnt, for the other portions were burnt on the altar, now to be eaten; the boiling, 'seethe his flesh in the holy place,' that the flesh was cooked in the holy place, made ready for the sacred meal of the priests; the meaning Wesley draws, that the priests were to eat the flesh and bread at the door of the tabernacle, to signify that God not only called them but fed them at his table; the type, that the priests' eating of the consecration flesh shadows the feeding on Christ, the believer's communion with God at his table; that God feeds those he consecrates to himself).

themes: thou shalt take the ram of the consecration
and seethe his flesh in the holy place
the flesh of the consecration ram boiled in the holy place for the priests to eat
God not only called them but fed them at his table, a type of feeding on Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the flesh of the ram was typical of the flesh of Christ, whose flesh is meat indeed, to be eaten by faith, whereby it becomes spiritual food, savoury and nourishing, to all the Lord's priests; the witnesses note God appointing Aaron and his sons to eat the flesh of the ram and the bread by the door of the tabernacle — the holy meal of the priests, feeding on the consecration offering (the priests eating of their offering before God: 'Aaron and his sons shall eat the flesh of the ram, and the bread that is in the basket, by the door of the tabernacle'; the eating, 'shall eat the flesh of the ram, and the bread,' that the priests fed on the consecration flesh and the bread, partaking of the holy meal; the place, 'by the door of the tabernacle,' before the LORD, in the holy place of his presence; the great type the commentators draw, that the flesh of the ram was typical of the flesh of Christ, whose flesh is meat indeed, to be eaten by faith, whereby it becomes spiritual food, savoury and nourishing, to all the Lord's priests, that is, all believers; that they who are consecrated to God feed on Christ, their sacrifice, by faith; that the believer's life and strength is in feeding on Christ).

debates.question: What does the priests' eating of the flesh of the consecration ram typify?

sides.position: Feeding on the flesh of Christ — 'whose flesh is meat indeed' (John 6:55), to be eaten by faith, whereby it becomes spiritual food, savoury and nourishing, to all the Lord's priests (all believers); the believer's life and strength is in feeding on Christ

themes: Aaron and his sons shall eat the flesh of the ram, and the bread that is in the basket
by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation
the priests eating the flesh and bread before the LORD, the holy meal
a type of feeding on the flesh of Christ, 'meat indeed,' by faith, the believer's spiritual food

schools.summary: Gill: they ate those things by which atonement was made for the sins of Aaron and his sons, for they were men of infirmity and needed sacrifice for themselves; herein Christ their antitype excelled, having no sin of his own; the witnesses note God appointing the priests to eat the things by which atonement was made, to consecrate and sanctify them, but forbidding a stranger to eat, because they are holy — the eating of the atoning offering, restricted to the consecrated (only the holy may eat the holy things: 'they shall eat those things wherewith the atonement was made, to consecrate and to sanctify them: but a stranger shall not eat thereof, because they are holy'; the eating, 'those things wherewith the atonement was made,' that the priests ate the very offerings by which their own atonement was made, for they were men of infirmity, and needed sacrifice for sin themselves; the contrast the commentators mark, that herein Christ their antitype excelled them, that he had no sin of his own, and needed not to offer first for himself; the purpose, 'to consecrate and to sanctify them,' the eating completing their consecration; the restriction, 'a stranger shall not eat thereof, because they are holy,' that none but the consecrated priests, none unhallowed, might eat the holy things; that holy things are for the holy, and that none feeds on Christ but the consecrated).

debates.question: How does Christ excel the Aaronic priests who ate the atoning offering?

sides.position: The priests ate the things by which their own atonement was made, for they were men of infirmity needing sacrifice for their own sin; but Christ their antitype had no sin of his own and needed not to offer first for himself (Hebrews 7:27)

themes: they shall eat those things wherewith the atonement was made, to consecrate and to sanctify them
but a stranger shall not eat thereof, because they are holy
the priests eating the atoning offering (men of infirmity, needing sacrifice for themselves), but no stranger
Christ excelling them, having no sin of his own; holy things for the holy, none feeds on Christ but the consecrated

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: whereas common, voluntary, and peace offerings might be kept and eaten on the morrow, the remainder of these holy things was to be burnt with fire; the witnesses note God appointing that any flesh or bread of the consecration left till morning be burnt with fire, not eaten, because it is holy — the burning of the holy remnant, that nothing holy be left to corruption or common use (the leftover holy things burnt: 'if ought of the flesh of the consecrations, or of the bread, remain unto the morning, then thou shalt burn the remainder with fire: it shall not be eaten, because it is holy'; the case, 'if ought... remain unto the morning,' being more than the priests could eat that day; the command, 'burn the remainder with fire... it shall not be eaten,' that, unlike common offerings, which might be eaten the next day, nothing of this holy consecration food was to be kept and eaten on the morrow; the reason, 'because it is holy,' that, being most holy, it must not be left to grow stale or be put to common use, but consumed with fire; the meaning, that holy things are to be treated as holy, nothing of God's portion left to corruption; that God will have his holy things kept holy).

themes: if ought of the flesh of the consecrations, or of the bread, remain unto the morning, then thou shalt burn the remainder with fire
it shall not be eaten, because it is holy
the remnant of the consecration food burnt with fire, not kept and eaten, because holy
holy things to be treated as holy, nothing of God's portion left to corruption or common use

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the consecration was to last seven days, the rite being repeated each day; the witnesses note God charging that all be done to Aaron and his sons according to all he had commanded, the consecration lasting seven days — the summing of the rite and the seven days' continuance (all to be done exactly, through seven days: 'thus shalt thou do unto Aaron, and to his sons, according to all things which I have commanded thee: seven days shalt thou consecrate them'; the charge of exactness, 'according to all things which I have commanded thee,' that the whole rite of consecration, washing, clothing, anointing, sprinkling blood, and offering sacrifice was to be done precisely as God ordered, nothing altered; the seven days, 'seven days shalt thou consecrate them,' that the consecration was not done in a day but continued seven days, the rite repeated each day; the meaning, that the seven days, the perfect number, marked a full and complete consecration, solemn and entire; that God would have his servants thoroughly and exactly consecrated, by his own rule; that nothing in God's service is to be done by halves or by man's invention).

themes: thus shalt thou do unto Aaron, and to his sons, according to all things which I have commanded thee
seven days shalt thou consecrate them
the whole rite to be done exactly as commanded, the consecration repeated seven days
the perfect number marking a full and complete consecration, God's servants thoroughly consecrated by his own rule

schools.summary: Gill: a bullock was to be offered every day of the seven days of consecration, denoting the full and complete atonement for sin by the sacrifice of Christ; the witnesses note God appointing a daily bullock for a sin offering during the seven days, to cleanse, atone for, and anoint the altar — the daily atonement of the seven days and the cleansing of the altar (atonement made each day for sin and for the altar: 'thou shalt offer every day a bullock for a sin offering for atonement: and thou shalt cleanse the altar... and... anoint it, to sanctify it'; the daily offering, 'every day a bullock for a sin offering for atonement,' that each of the seven days a bullock was offered, making atonement; the meaning the commentators draw, that this daily atonement, repeated, denotes the full and complete atonement for sin by the sacrifice of Christ; the altar, 'cleanse the altar... when thou hast made an atonement for it, and... anoint it, to sanctify it,' that the altar itself was cleansed, atoned for, and anointed, that it might be holy and fit for the offerings; that even the altar must be hallowed by atonement and anointing; that all is cleansed and sanctified by atoning blood).

themes: thou shalt offer every day a bullock for a sin offering for atonement
and thou shalt cleanse the altar... and thou shalt anoint it, to sanctify it
a bullock offered each of the seven days, the altar cleansed, atoned for, and anointed
the daily atonement denoting the full and complete atonement for sin by the sacrifice of Christ

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: to 'whatsoever toucheth the altar shall be holy' our Lord refers, asking whether the gift or the altar that sanctifieth the gift be greater; the witnesses note God appointing seven days' atonement for the altar, that it be most holy, so that whatever touched it was holy — the full hallowing of the altar and its sanctifying virtue (the altar made most holy, hallowing all it touches: 'Seven days thou shalt make an atonement for the altar, and sanctify it; and it shall be an altar most holy: whatsoever toucheth the altar shall be holy'; the seven days, 'seven days thou shalt make an atonement for the altar,' that the altar was atoned for and sanctified each of the seven days, that it might be thoroughly fit to have sacrifices offered on it; the result, 'it shall be an altar most holy,' that the altar was made most holy by this atonement; the virtue, 'whatsoever toucheth the altar shall be holy,' that the altar so hallowed sanctified whatever was laid on it, the gift made holy by the altar; the reference our Lord made, the altar that sanctifieth the gift, the altar being greater than the gift; the type, that Christ is the altar that sanctifies all our offerings, making them acceptable to God).

debates.question: What does 'whatsoever toucheth the altar shall be holy' point to?

sides.position: The altar's sanctifying virtue, to which our Lord referred (Matthew 23:19) — 'the altar that sanctifieth the gift,' the altar being greater than the gift; a type of Christ, the altar that sanctifies all our offerings, making them acceptable to God

themes: Seven days thou shalt make an atonement for the altar, and sanctify it; and it shall be an altar most holy
whatsoever toucheth the altar shall be holy
the altar atoned for seven days, made most holy, hallowing whatever touched it
a type of Christ, the altar that sanctifies all our offerings, making them acceptable to God

schools.summary: Gill: the altar being built, sanctified, and the priests consecrated, an account is given of the daily sacrifice, two lambs of the first year, day by day; the witnesses note God appointing two lambs of the first year to be offered upon the altar day by day continually — the institution of the daily sacrifice, the continual offering (two lambs offered every day for ever: 'this is that which thou shalt offer upon the altar; two lambs of the first year day by day continually'; the occasion, that, the altar built and sanctified and the priests consecrated, God now appoints the daily sacrifice; the victims, 'two lambs of the first year,' young lambs, without blemish, the daily offering; the continuance, 'day by day continually,' that this was not a single offering but a perpetual one, made every day, morning and evening; the type the commentators draw, that the daily lamb shadows Christ, the Lamb of God, whose one sacrifice is of perpetual and continual virtue, ever pleaded before God, and the church's continual worship and dependence on him; that God's people are to live in continual offering and communion).

debates.question: What does the continual daily lamb typify?

sides.position: Christ, the Lamb of God — whose one sacrifice is of perpetual and continual virtue, ever pleaded before God — and the church's continual worship and dependence on him

themes: this is that which thou shalt offer upon the altar
two lambs of the first year day by day continually
the institution of the daily sacrifice, two lambs offered upon the altar continually
a type of Christ the Lamb, whose sacrifice is of continual virtue, and the church's continual worship

schools.summary: Gill: the one lamb was to be offered in the morning, before which no other sacrifice was offered, and so slain very early, yet not before break of day; the witnesses note God appointing one lamb to be offered in the morning and the other at evening — the morning and evening sacrifice, the bounding of each day with worship (the day begun and ended with an offering: 'The one lamb thou shalt offer in the morning; and the other lamb thou shalt offer at even'; the morning lamb, 'the one lamb... in the morning,' offered first, before which no other sacrifice was to be offered, slain very early, yet not before break of day; the evening lamb, 'the other lamb... at even,' offered at the close of the day; that thus each day was begun and ended with a sacrifice, the worship of God bounding the whole day; the meaning the commentators draw, that the morning and evening lamb teaches the duty of beginning and ending every day with God, in worship and in pleading the sacrifice of Christ; the type, that Christ, the Lamb, is our morning and evening sacrifice, our dependence at all times; that we are to begin and close each day with him).

themes: The one lamb thou shalt offer in the morning
and the other lamb thou shalt offer at even
one lamb offered in the morning and the other at evening, the day bounded with worship
a teaching to begin and end every day with God, pleading Christ our morning and evening Lamb

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: a tenth deal of flour, the tenth part of an ephah, with beaten oil and wine, accompanied the daily lamb as a meat and drink offering; the witnesses note God appointing a tenth deal of flour mingled with oil, and a fourth of a hin of wine, to be offered with each lamb — the meat and drink offerings that accompanied the daily lamb (bread, oil, and wine going with the sacrifice: 'with the one lamb a tenth deal of flour mingled with the fourth part of an hin of beaten oil; and the fourth part of an hin of wine for a drink offering'; the meat offering, 'a tenth deal of flour mingled with... beaten oil,' fine flour, an omer's worth, mingled with the best beaten oil, the meat offering; the drink offering, 'the fourth part of an hin of wine for a drink offering,' wine poured out with the sacrifice; that the daily lamb was not offered alone but with bread, oil, and wine, the full provision of an offering; the meaning the commentators draw, that the flour, oil, and wine, joined with the lamb, shadow Christ and the fulness that is in him, the bread of life, the oil of the Spirit, and the wine of joy and his blood; that our offering of ourselves is to be full and complete).

themes: with the one lamb a tenth deal of flour mingled with the fourth part of an hin of beaten oil
and the fourth part of an hin of wine for a drink offering
the meat offering of flour and oil and the drink offering of wine joined to the daily lamb
some seeing the flour, oil, and wine shadow Christ and the fulness in him, the bread, the Spirit, and the wine of joy

schools.summary: Jamieson, Fausset and Brown: the mere fact of such a multitude being fed for forty years in the wilderness, where no food is to be obtained, is itself a standing miracle; the witnesses note God appointing the evening lamb to be offered with the same meat and drink offering as the morning, a sweet savour to the LORD — the evening sacrifice answering the morning, both well-pleasing (the evening offering matching the morning: 'the other lamb thou shalt offer at even, and shalt do thereto according to the meat offering of the morning... for a sweet savour, an offering made by fire unto the LORD'; the evening lamb, 'the other lamb thou shalt offer at even,' offered at the day's close; the same provision, 'according to the meat offering of the morning, and according to the drink offering thereof,' that the evening offering had the same flour, oil, and wine as the morning, the two answering each other; the acceptance, 'for a sweet savour, an offering made by fire unto the LORD,' that both, morning and evening, were well-pleasing and accepted of God; the constancy, that God was worshipped morning and evening alike, the whole day compassed with sacrifice; the type, that Christ's sacrifice is a sweet savour at all times, and our worship is to be constant and accepted in him).

themes: the other lamb thou shalt offer at even, and shalt do thereto according to the meat offering of the morning
for a sweet savour, an offering made by fire unto the LORD
the evening lamb offered with the same provision as the morning, a sweet savour to the LORD
the whole day compassed with sacrifice, our worship constant and accepted in Christ

schools.summary: Gill: this was to be a continual burnt offering, offered up morning and evening in every age, as long as the Mosaic economy lasted, till he came who put an end to it by offering up himself; the witnesses note God making this a continual burnt offering throughout their generations, at the door of the tabernacle, where he would meet and speak to them — the perpetuity of the daily sacrifice and the promise of God's meeting (the offering for ever and God meeting them there: 'a continual burnt offering throughout your generations at the door of the tabernacle... before the LORD: where I will meet you, to speak there unto thee'; the perpetuity, 'a continual burnt offering throughout your generations,' that this daily offering was to continue in every age till he came who put an end to it by offering up himself; the place, 'at the door of the tabernacle... before the LORD,' where the sacrifice was offered and God was approached; the promise, 'where I will meet you, to speak there unto thee,' that God would there meet his people and speak to them, the place of sacrifice being the place of meeting and communion; the type, that it is through the continual sacrifice of Christ that God meets and speaks with his people, communion grounded on atonement; that God meets his people at the place of the offering).

themes: This shall be a continual burnt offering throughout your generations at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation before the LORD
where I will meet you, to speak there unto thee
the continual burnt offering for ever, till Christ ended it by offering himself, the place of God's meeting
through his continual sacrifice God meets and speaks with his people, communion grounded on atonement

schools.summary: Gill: God would meet not only with Moses or Aaron, but with the people themselves, granting them his gracious presence in public ordinances, and the tabernacle sanctified by his glory; the witnesses note God promising to meet with the children of Israel there, and that the tabernacle should be sanctified by his glory — the promise of God's gracious presence with his people and his glory hallowing the place (God meeting his whole people and his glory sanctifying the tent: 'there I will meet with the children of Israel, and the tabernacle shall be sanctified by my glory'; the meeting, 'I will meet with the children of Israel,' that God would meet not only with Moses or with Aaron, but with the people themselves, granting them his gracious presence in public ordinances; the sanctifying, 'the tabernacle shall be sanctified by my glory,' that the place would be hallowed by the appearing of God's glory, as when the cloud filled it; that God's presence and glory are what make a place holy, not the building itself; the comfort, that God meets his people in his appointed ordinances and fills them with his glory; the type, that God in Christ meets and dwells with his people, and his glory fills his true temple; that the presence of God is the glory of his house).

themes: there I will meet with the children of Israel
and the tabernacle shall be sanctified by my glory
God's promise to meet with the people themselves and his glory hallowing the tabernacle
God's presence and glory, not the building, what makes a place holy, the presence of God the glory of his house

schools.summary: Gill: this intimates that something else is intended as what should be sanctified, even God himself sanctifying the tabernacle, altar, and priests, beyond what man could do; the witnesses note God promising to sanctify the tabernacle, the altar, and Aaron and his sons, to minister to him — the divine hallowing of place, altar, and priests, that all is God's own work (God himself sanctifying all for his service: 'I will sanctify the tabernacle of the congregation, and the altar: I will sanctify also both Aaron and his sons, to minister to me in the priest's office'; the threefold sanctifying, 'the tabernacle... the altar... both Aaron and his sons,' the place, the altar, and the priests all hallowed; the great word, 'I will sanctify,' that, beyond all the outward rites of consecration, it was God himself who would truly sanctify, the real hallowing being his own work; the end, 'to minister to me in the priest's office,' that all was sanctified for God's service; the meaning the commentators draw, that man's rites cannot truly hallow, it is God who sanctifies, the outward consecration being but the sign of his inward work; the type, that it is God who sanctifies his people and their service, through Christ and the Spirit; that all true holiness is God's gift).

themes: I will sanctify the tabernacle of the congregation, and the altar
I will sanctify also both Aaron and his sons, to minister to me in the priest's office
God promising himself to sanctify the tabernacle, the altar, and the priests for his service
the real hallowing God's own work; man's rites cannot truly hallow, all true holiness God's gift through Christ and the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: God would dwell among the children of Israel in the tabernacle ordered to be built, placed in the midst of the camp, where Jehovah dwelt as a king in his palace; the witnesses note God promising to dwell among the children of Israel and to be their God — the great end of the tabernacle and all its service, that God might dwell with his people (God himself coming to dwell among them: 'I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will be their God'; the dwelling, 'I will dwell among the children of Israel,' that God would dwell in the tabernacle, placed in the midst of the camp, where Jehovah dwelt as a king in his palace, in the midst of his people; the covenant, 'and will be their God,' that he would own them as his and be to them all that God is to his people; that this was the great end of the tabernacle and all the priesthood and sacrifices, that the holy God might dwell among a sinful people, sin atoned and the way opened; the great type the commentators draw, that this is fulfilled in Christ, the Word made flesh, who dwelt, tabernacled, among us, in Immanuel, God with us, and at last in the new Jerusalem, the tabernacle of God is with men; that God dwelling with his people is the sum of all blessing).

debates.question: What is the great end of the tabernacle and all its service?

sides.position: That God might dwell among his people and be their God — the holy God dwelling among a sinful people, sin atoned and the way opened; fulfilled in Christ, the Word made flesh who tabernacled among us (John 1:14), in Immanuel, and at last in the new Jerusalem, 'the tabernacle of God is with men' (Revelation 21:3)

themes: And I will dwell among the children of Israel
and will be their God
God's great promise to dwell among Israel and be their God, the very end of the tabernacle
fulfilled in Christ, the Word made flesh who tabernacled among us, in Immanuel, and at last 'the tabernacle of God is with men'

schools.summary: Gill: they should know him by his presence with them, by the blessings bestowed, and by his care and kindness, the LORD their God who brought them out of Egypt; the witnesses note God declaring that Israel should know him as the LORD their God, who brought them out of Egypt that he might dwell among them — the end of the deliverance and the tabernacle, that God might dwell with a people who know him (the very purpose of the redemption being that God might dwell among them: 'they shall know that I am the LORD their God, that brought them forth out of the land of Egypt, that I may dwell among them'; the knowing, 'they shall know that I am the LORD their God,' that, by his presence, the blessings bestowed, his care and kindness, they should know him truly as their God; the deliverance, 'that brought them forth out of the land of Egypt,' that the great redemption from Egypt was the proof and ground of his being their God; the great purpose, 'that I may dwell among them,' that God redeemed them out of Egypt not merely to free them, but that I may dwell among them, the end of the redemption being his dwelling with them; the type, that Christ redeems his people from a worse bondage that he may dwell in and among them by his Spirit; the seal, 'I am the LORD their God,' the great name repeated, sealing the covenant; that God redeems his people that he may dwell with them for ever).

debates.question: Why did God bring Israel out of Egypt?

sides.position: That he might dwell among them — the great purpose of the redemption being not merely to free them but that God might dwell with his people; a type of Christ, who redeems his people from a worse bondage that he may dwell in and among them by his Spirit, that God may dwell with his people for ever

themes: they shall know that I am the LORD their God, that brought them forth out of the land of Egypt, that I may dwell among them
I am the LORD their God
Israel knowing the LORD, who redeemed them out of Egypt that he might dwell among them
the great purpose of the redemption his dwelling with them, a type of Christ who redeems to dwell within his people
